{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.

{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Sources === {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Waka|ja|和歌}} * {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌}} * {{ill|List of poets|ja|歌人一覧}}

{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Sources === {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Waka|ja|和歌}} * {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌}} * {{ill|List of poets|ja|歌人一覧}}

{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Sources === {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Waka|ja|和歌}} * {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌}} * {{ill|List of poets|ja|歌人一覧}}

Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.

{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.

{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.

{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Sources === {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Waka|ja|和歌}} * {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌}} * {{ill|List of poets|ja|歌人一覧}}

revision.text:
{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Sources === {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Waka|ja|和歌}} * {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌}} * {{ill|List of poets|ja|歌人一覧}}

[[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Waka poet}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Waka poet}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]] <!--Waka poet-->[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:歌人]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:aka poet]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Waka poet]][[Category:qq]]

{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.

{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.

{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.

{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.
== References ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist}}== See Also == * {{ill|Waka|ja|和歌}} * {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌}} * {{ill|List of poets|ja|歌人一覧}}

{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.
== References ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Waka|ja|和歌}} * {{ill|Tanka|ja|短歌}} * {{ill|List of poets|ja|歌人一覧}}

{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|waka|ja|和歌}} or {{ill|tanka|ja|短歌}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|ja|柿本人麻呂}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|ja|紀貫之}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|ja|古今和歌集}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|ja|山部赤人}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神}} and {{ill|Otō-no-Hime|ja|衣通姫}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Saitō Mokichi|ja|斎藤茂吉}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{要出典|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|ja|歌会始}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Six Poetic Immortals|ja|六歌仙}}, five are included in the {{ill|Hyakunin Isshu|ja|百人一首}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|ja|三十六歌仙}}.
== References ==

revision.text:
{{otheruses|歌人（'''かじん'''）|村下孝蔵のアルバムの歌人（'''うたびと'''）|歌人〜ソングコレクション〜}} {{出典の明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''歌人'''（かじん）とは、[[和歌]]または[[短歌]]を詠む人物のことをいう。'''歌詠み'''（うたよみ）ともいう<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>。
== 解説 == 現代において歌人というのは日常的に和歌・短歌を作り、何らかの手段でそれを発表している人を指す場合が普通である。しかし、近代以前には歌人というのが歌を詠むことだけを生業としている人々だったわけではない。古代や中古以降において「歌詠み」と呼ばれる人々は、[[皇族]]や[[出家]]し[[僧侶]]の身分になった者は別として、朝廷より[[官位]]を得ている官人や朝廷や[[幕府]]などに仕える[[武家]]でありその妻や娘たちであった。[[柿本人麻呂]]は「宮廷歌人」であったなどといわれるが、俗称であり「宮廷歌人」なる官職は無かった。人麻呂もその詳細については不明ながら、朝廷より何らかの官位を得ていた官人であったと見られ、[[紀貫之]]についても同様に官位を得、普段はその職務に従う官人であった。つまりは当時はいかに和歌において高名をなそうとも、職業としての「歌人」というものはありえなかったのである。
古くはそれら歌人の中でも、特に和歌に優れた人物のことを'''歌聖'''（かせい）と呼んだ<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>。それは単なる敬意や尊称をあらわすのみならず、[[歌道]]において[[神]]としてあがめられる歌人を指しており、具体的には『[[古今和歌集]]』の仮名序の記述から柿本人麻呂と[[山部赤人]]を指す。歌道において神とされるのは人麻呂や赤人のほかに[[住吉明神]]や[[衣通姫]]などいくつかあり、それらから三つを撰んで'''和歌三神'''（わかさんじん）と称することがあった<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>。近代短歌では歌聖に[[斎藤茂吉]]を挙げることもある。
ただし『古今和歌集』仮名序の解釈では、「歌聖」は柿本人麻呂ただ一人とする説もある。その場合、山部赤人は「[[歌仙]]」とされる。根拠は人麻呂の記述「正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける」に対し、赤人の記述「また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり」と記述に切り替えがあることと、「なりける」と「ありけり」の解釈が「聖だった」と「人がいた」とすることによる。しかし、その後に「人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける」との記述から、歌人としては同列{{要出典|date=2015年9月}}と見做し、赤人も歌聖であるとする説もある。
近代以降の歌人の多くは[[結社#文学における結社|短歌結社]]に所属し、その結社の[[雑誌]]に作品を発表している。特に、その短歌、[[歌論]]、歌集書評に対して稿料・印税などが発生したり、歌に関する[[講演]]・[[批評]]・[[教育]]・[[啓蒙]]・選歌活動に対して[[報酬]]が発生したりすることが日常的になった場合、「専門歌人」という。ただし、多くの高名な「専門歌人」でも歌人としての活動だけで生活するのが困難であるため、「プロ歌人」という呼称は使われていない。「専門歌人」に対し、もっぱら[[新聞]]などの投稿欄に作品を寄せている歌人も多く、その場合は「投稿歌人」「新聞歌人」などという。また、[[インターネット]]の[[ホームページ]]や[[ブログ]]に作品を発表する「ネット歌人」も現れてきている。
歌人たちにおける社会を'''[[歌壇]]'''（かだん）ということがある。また公的な機関では皇族へ短歌を指南する宮内庁御用掛という役職が存在しており、宮中[[歌会始]]で宮内庁へ応募されてきた作品を選考する選者も存在する。
=== 補記 === [[六歌仙]]から[[百人一首]]に選ばれているのは5人、[[三十六歌仙]]からは15人である。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <!--=== 注釈 === {{Notelist}}--> === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[和歌]] * [[短歌]] * [[歌人一覧]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11122954]] Wikidata name: waka poet [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes [[:en:Waka (poetry)|waka]] or [[:en:Tanka|tanka]]. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior families|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shogunate|WD=Q1074150}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial|qq=|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}} or monastic status. For example, while [[:en:Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]] is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court Rank. Similarly, [[:en:Ki no Tsurayuki|Ki no Tsurayuki]] was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kado|qq=|1=kadō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545302}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the [[:en:Kokin Wakashū|Kokin Wakashū]], it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and [[:en:Yamabe no Akahito|Yamabe no Akahito]]. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}} and [[:en:Princess Sotoorihime|Otō-no-Hime]]. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, [[:en:Mokichi Saitō|Saitō Mokichi]] is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].
