== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior families|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shogunate|WD=Q1074150}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}} or monastic status. For example, while [[:en:Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]] is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court Rank. Similarly, [[:en:Ki no Tsurayuki|Ki no Tsurayuki]] was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kado|1=kadō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545302}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the [[:en:Kokin Wakashū|Kokin Wakashū]], it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and [[:en:Yamabe no Akahito|Yamabe no Akahito]]. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}} and [[:en:Princess Sotoorihime|Otō-no-Hime]]. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, [[:en:Mokichi Saitō|Saitō Mokichi]] is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].

Similarly, [[:en:Ki no Tsurayuki|Ki no Tsurayuki]] was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kado|1=kadō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545302}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the [[:en:Kokin Wakashū|Kokin Wakashū]], it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and [[:en:Yamabe no Akahito|Yamabe no Akahito]]. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}} and [[:en:Princess Sotoorihime|Otō-no-Hime]]. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, [[:en:Mokichi Saitō|Saitō Mokichi]] is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11122954]] Wikidata name: waka poet [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes [[:en:Waka (poetry)|waka]] or [[:en:Tanka|tanka]]. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior families|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shogunate|WD=Q1074150}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}} or monastic status. For example, while [[:en:Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]] is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court Rank. Similarly, [[:en:Ki no Tsurayuki|Ki no Tsurayuki]] was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kado|1=kadō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545302}} (the way of waka). From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, [[:en:Mokichi Saitō|Saitō Mokichi]] is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, [[:en:Mokichi Saitō|Saitō Mokichi]] is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11122954]] Wikidata name: waka poet [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes [[:en:Waka (poetry)|waka]] or [[:en:Tanka|tanka]]. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior families|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shogunate|WD=Q1074150}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}} or monastic status. For example, while [[:en:Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]] is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court Rank. Similarly, [[:en:Ki no Tsurayuki|Ki no Tsurayuki]] was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kado|1=kadō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545302}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the [[:en:Kokin Wakashū|Kokin Wakashū]], it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and [[:en:Yamabe no Akahito|Yamabe no Akahito]]. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}} and [[:en:Princess Sotoorihime|Otō-no-Hime]]. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, [[:en:Mokichi Saitō|Saitō Mokichi]] is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].

Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the [[:en:Kokin Wakashū|Kokin Wakashū]], it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and [[:en:Yamabe no Akahito|Yamabe no Akahito]]. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, [[:en:Mokichi Saitō|Saitō Mokichi]] is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11122954]] Wikidata name: waka poet [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes [[:en:Waka (poetry)|waka]] or [[:en:Tanka|tanka]]. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior families|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shogunate|WD=Q1074150}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}} or monastic status. For example, while [[:en:Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]] is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court Rank. Similarly, [[:en:Ki no Tsurayuki|Ki no Tsurayuki]] was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kado|1=kadō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545302}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the [[:en:Kokin Wakashū|Kokin Wakashū]], it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and [[:en:Yamabe no Akahito|Yamabe no Akahito]]. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}} and [[:en:Princess Sotoorihime|Otō-no-Hime]]. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, [[:en:Mokichi Saitō|Saitō Mokichi]] is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].

Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the [[:en:Kokin Wakashū|Kokin Wakashū]], it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and [[:en:Yamabe no Akahito|Yamabe no Akahito]]. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}} and [[:en:Princess Sotoorihime|Otō-no-Hime]]. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, [[:en:Mokichi Saitō|Saitō Mokichi]] is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11122954]] Wikidata name: waka poet [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes [[:en:Waka (poetry)|waka]] or [[:en:Tanka|tanka]]. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior families|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shogunate|WD=Q1074150}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}} or monastic status. For example, while [[:en:Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]] is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court Rank. Similarly, [[:en:Ki no Tsurayuki|Ki no Tsurayuki]] was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kado|1=kadō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545302}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the [[:en:Kokin Wakashū|Kokin Wakashū]], it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and [[:en:Yamabe no Akahito|Yamabe no Akahito]]. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}} and [[:en:Princess Sotoorihime|Otō-no-Hime]]. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, [[:en:Mokichi Saitō|Saitō Mokichi]] is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].

From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, [[:en:Mokichi Saitō|Saitō Mokichi]] is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.

One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11122954]] Wikidata name: waka poet [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes [[:en:Waka (poetry)|waka]] or [[:en:Tanka|tanka]]. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior families|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shogunate|WD=Q1074150}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}} or monastic status. For example, while [[:en:Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]] is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court Rank. Similarly, [[:en:Ki no Tsurayuki|Ki no Tsurayuki]] was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kado|1=kadō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545302}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the [[:en:Kokin Wakashū|Kokin Wakashū]], it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and [[:en:Yamabe no Akahito|Yamabe no Akahito]]. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}} and [[:en:Princess Sotoorihime|Otō-no-Hime]]. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, [[:en:Mokichi Saitō|Saitō Mokichi]] is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].

This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11122954]] Wikidata name: waka poet [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes [[:en:Waka (poetry)|waka]] or [[:en:Tanka|tanka]]. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior families|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court]] or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shogunate|WD=Q1074150}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}} or monastic status. For example, while [[:en:Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]] is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court Rank. Similarly, [[:en:Ki no Tsurayuki|Ki no Tsurayuki]] was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kado|1=kadō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545302}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the [[:en:Kokin Wakashū|Kokin Wakashū]], it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and [[:en:Yamabe no Akahito|Yamabe no Akahito]]. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}} and [[:en:Princess Sotoorihime|Otō-no-Hime]]. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, [[:en:Mokichi Saitō|Saitō Mokichi]] is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].

In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial [[:en:Utakai Hajime|Utakai Hajime]].
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the [[:en:Rokkasen|Six Poetic Immortals]], five are included in the [[:en:Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|Hyakunin Isshu]], and 15 are drawn from the [[:en:Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry]].

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11122954]] Wikidata name: waka poet [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|Waka (poetry)|en|Waka (poetry)|WD=Q737148|lt=waka}} or {{ill|Tanka|en|Tanka|WD=Q258331|lt=tanka}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior families|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shogunate|WD=Q1074150}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|en|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|WD=Q380818|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court Rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|en|Ki no Tsurayuki|WD=Q468288|lt=Ki no Tsurayuki}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kado|1=kadō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545302}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|en|Kokin Wakashū|WD=Q1047167|lt=Kokin Wakashū}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|en|Yamabe no Akahito|WD=Q180929|lt=Yamabe no Akahito}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}} and {{ill|Princess Sotoorihime|en|Princess Sotoorihime|WD=Q11626419|lt=Otō-no-Hime}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Mokichi Saitō|en|Mokichi Saitō|WD=Q3319377|lt=Saitō Mokichi}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|en|Utakai Hajime|WD=Q758946|lt=Utakai Hajime}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Rokkasen|en|Rokkasen|WD=Q1336997|lt=Six Poetic Immortals}}, five are included in the {{ill|Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|en|Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|WD=Q1145042|lt=Hyakunin Isshu}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|en|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|WD=Q671039|lt=Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry}}.

Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|en|Kokin Wakashū|WD=Q1047167|lt=Kokin Wakashū}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|en|Yamabe no Akahito|WD=Q180929|lt=Yamabe no Akahito}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}} and {{ill|Princess Sotoorihime|en|Princess Sotoorihime|WD=Q11626419|lt=Otō-no-Hime}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Mokichi Saitō|en|Mokichi Saitō|WD=Q3319377|lt=Saitō Mokichi}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|en|Utakai Hajime|WD=Q758946|lt=Utakai Hajime}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Rokkasen|en|Rokkasen|WD=Q1336997|lt=Six Poetic Immortals}}, five are included in the {{ill|Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|en|Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|WD=Q1145042|lt=Hyakunin Isshu}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|en|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|WD=Q671039|lt=Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:歌人]][[ko:가인 (호칭)]][[zh:歌人]]
{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|Waka (poetry)|en|Waka (poetry)|WD=Q737148|lt=waka}} or {{ill|Tanka|en|Tanka|WD=Q258331|lt=tanka}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior families|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shogunate|WD=Q1074150}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|en|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|WD=Q380818|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court Rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|en|Ki no Tsurayuki|WD=Q468288|lt=Ki no Tsurayuki}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kado|1=kadō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545302}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|en|Kokin Wakashū|WD=Q1047167|lt=Kokin Wakashū}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|en|Yamabe no Akahito|WD=Q180929|lt=Yamabe no Akahito}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}} and {{ill|Princess Sotoorihime|en|Princess Sotoorihime|WD=Q11626419|lt=Otō-no-Hime}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Mokichi Saitō|en|Mokichi Saitō|WD=Q3319377|lt=Saitō Mokichi}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|en|Utakai Hajime|WD=Q758946|lt=Utakai Hajime}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Rokkasen|en|Rokkasen|WD=Q1336997|lt=Six Poetic Immortals}}, five are included in the {{ill|Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|en|Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|WD=Q1145042|lt=Hyakunin Isshu}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|en|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|WD=Q671039|lt=Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry}}.

For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|en|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|WD=Q380818|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court Rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|en|Ki no Tsurayuki|WD=Q468288|lt=Ki no Tsurayuki|1=Ki no Tsurayuki}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
Among these poets, those particularly skilled in waka were referred to as '''Kasei''' (歌聖).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌聖|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> This term not only expressed respect but also referred to poets worshiped as gods in the field of {{ill|kado|ja|歌道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kado|1=kadō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545302}} (the way of waka). Specifically, based on descriptions in the preface to the {{ill|Kokin Wakashū|en|Kokin Wakashū|WD=Q1047167|lt=Kokin Wakashū|1=Kokin Wakashū}}, it included Kakinomoto no Hitomaro and {{ill|Yamabe no Akahito|en|Yamabe no Akahito|WD=Q180929|lt=Yamabe no Akahito|1=Yamabe no Akahito}}. Other figures regarded as gods in kado included {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}} and {{ill|Princess Sotoorihime|en|Princess Sotoorihime|WD=Q11626419|lt=Otō-no-Hime|1=Princess Sotoorihime}}. From these, three were sometimes chosen as the '''Three Gods of Waka''' (和歌三神).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=和歌三神|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、歌舞伎・浄瑠璃外題よみかた辞典、デジタル大辞泉プラス|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref> In modern tanka, {{ill|Mokichi Saitō|en|Mokichi Saitō|WD=Q3319377|lt=Saitō Mokichi|1=Mokichi Saitō}} is sometimes referred to as a Kasei.
However, interpretations of the preface to the Kokin Wakashū differ. One view holds that "Kasei" refers solely to Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, with Yamabe no Akahito considered a {{ill|Kasen|ja|歌仙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (poetic sage). This interpretation is based on differences in the text: Hitomaro is described as "正三位柿本人麿なむ歌の聖なりける" ("Kakinomoto no Hitomaro, Senior Third Rank, was a saint of poetry"), while Akahito is introduced with "また山の辺赤人といふ人ありけり" ("There was also a man named Yamabe no Akahito"). The phrases "なりける" ("was a saint") and "ありけり" ("was there") are interpreted as distinct. Nonetheless, later text states, "人麿は赤人が上に立たむことかたく 赤人は人麿が下に立たむことかたくなむありける" ("Hitomaro could not be placed above Akahito, nor could Akahito be placed below Hitomaro"), suggesting that they are of equal standing as poets.{{Citation needed|date=2015th year 9月}}
In modern times, many poets belong to {{ill|tanka societies|ja|結社#文学における結社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanka societies|qq=}} and publish their works in magazines associated with these groups. When their tanka, poetic theories, or book reviews generate royalties, or when they receive payment for lectures, critiques, education, public awareness campaigns, or poetry selection activities, they are often called "specialist poets" (専門歌人). However, even many renowned "specialist poets" find it difficult to make a living solely from their poetry, so the term "professional poet" (プロ歌人) is not commonly used. Conversely, those who primarily submit works to newspaper columns are called "newspaper poets" (新聞歌人) or "contributing poets" (投稿歌人). Recently, "net poets" (ネット歌人) have also emerged, publishing their works on personal websites and blogs.
The community of poets is sometimes referred to as the '''Kadan''' (歌壇). In official institutions, there is a position called {{ill|Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja|宮内庁御用掛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency Goyōkake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which involves instructing the imperial family in tanka. Additionally, selectors exist to review submissions for the Imperial {{ill|Utakai Hajime|en|Utakai Hajime|WD=Q758946|lt=Utakai Hajime|1=Utakai Hajime}}.
=== Supplementary Notes === Of the {{ill|Rokkasen|en|Rokkasen|WD=Q1336997|lt=Six Poetic Immortals|1=Rokkasen}}, five are included in the {{ill|Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|en|Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|WD=Q1145042|lt=Hyakunin Isshu|1=Ogura Hyakunin Isshu}}, and 15 are drawn from the {{ill|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|en|Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|WD=Q671039|lt=Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry|1=Thirty-Six Immortals of Poetry}}.

{{otheruses|Poet ('''Kajin''')|Murashita Kōzō's album '''Utabito'''|Utabito~Song Collection~}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2022-11-11}} '''Kajin''' (歌人) refers to a person who composes {{ill|Waka (poetry)|en|Waka (poetry)|WD=Q737148|lt=waka|1=Waka (poetry)}} or {{ill|Tanka|en|Tanka|WD=Q258331|lt=tanka|1=Tanka}}. It is also known as '''Utayomi''' (歌詠み).<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=歌人|encyclopedia=精選版 日本国語大辞典、デジタル大辞泉、普及版 字通|accessdate=2022-11-11}}</ref>
== Explanation == In modern times, the term "Kajin" usually refers to individuals who create waka or tanka on a regular basis and publish their work through various means. However, prior to the modern era, "Kajin" were not exclusively people who made a living solely from composing poetry. In ancient and medieval periods, those referred to as "Utayomi" often held positions as {{ill|government officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|warrior families|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior families|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Buke|WD=Q2927809}}, or their wives and daughters, serving the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court|1=Royal court}} or the {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shogunate|WD=Q1074150}}, except for those of {{ill|imperial|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}} or monastic status. For example, while {{ill|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|en|Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|WD=Q380818|lt=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro|1=Kakinomoto no Hitomaro}} is sometimes called a "court poet," this is a colloquial term, as there was no such official position. Hitomaro, whose exact role remains unclear, is believed to have been a government official who received a court Rank. Similarly, {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|en|Ki no Tsurayuki|WD=Q468288|lt=Ki no Tsurayuki|1=Ki no Tsurayuki}} was a government official who performed administrative duties in addition to composing waka. Thus, even if someone achieved fame in waka, the profession of "Kajin" did not exist at the time.
